behaviors.description: The request sent to the external API always includes the complete list of valid taxonomy labels.
behaviors.justification: This is a pure function call verification. Mock the external API client and assert that the module called it with the taxonomy labels parameter containing all expected labels. The mock captures the function call signature, so we can definitively verify what was passed. No I/O or external dependency behavior affects this.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API client. Call classify(document). Assert that the client's method was called with the taxonomy labels (e.g., ['legal', 'technical', 'financial', 'other']) passed as an argument.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A mock verifies the function call arguments. Serialization bugs (if labels get corrupted to JSON) would be the HTTP client's responsibility. The module's responsibility is to pass the correct data structure, which a mock verifies.

behaviors.description: When the API returns a label that is not in the taxonomy, the module returns "unknown" instead of forwarding the unexpected value to the caller.
behaviors.justification: This is validation logic inside the module. Mock the API to return an invalid label (e.g., 'garbage'). Verify that the module checks the label against the taxonomy and returns 'unknown' instead. A mock fully exercises the validation path.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to return {'label': 'invalid_label'}. Call classify(document). Assert that the return value is 'unknown', not 'invalid_label'.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The module owns this validation logic entirely. A mock can trigger all code paths. No external behavior needed.

behaviors.description: The classification result for the same document is the same across repeated calls.
behaviors.justification: A mock can verify that the module caches results. Call classify(doc) twice. Assert that the API client was called only once (second call returns the cached result). This assumes the module implements caching; if the module relies on API determinism instead, a System test would be needed, but caching is the typical design.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API. Call classify(document) twice with the same input. Assert that the API client's method was called exactly once, verifying the second call was served from cache.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: This test assumes the module implements caching. If the module instead relies on the external API being deterministic (no caching), a Unit mock would always pass because the mock returns the same result every time. In that case, a System test against the real API would be needed to catch non-determinism.

behaviors.description: The number of tokens consumed for a typical document stays within the configured per-call budget.
behaviors.justification: This asserts a property of actual token consumption. The module might truncate documents to stay within budget, which a Unit test can verify (mock shows truncation occurred). However, a Unit test cannot verify that a real document, when truncated and sent to the real API, actually stays within the budget. A mock would return a fixed token count (e.g., always return 400 tokens). A real API might consume 600 tokens for the same truncated document due to unpredictable token-per-character ratios or API implementation details. Only a System test with the real API can verify the assertion holds in practice.
behaviors.test_description: Send a typical document to the real external API with a configured token budget (e.g., 1000 tokens). Observe the actual token count in the API response. Assert that tokens_consumed ≤ budget.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: A Unit test can verify the module's truncation logic (necessary condition), but cannot verify the assertion about real token consumption. A mock always returns the expected count, hiding real failures where the API consumes more tokens than anticipated. System test catches this.

behaviors.description: When the API signals that the request rate is too high, the module waits and retries rather than returning an error to the caller.
behaviors.justification: This is retry logic within the module. Mock the API to return a rate-limit error on the first call and success on the second. Verify that the module calls time.sleep (mock it to avoid real delays) and retries. The entire error-handling and retry path can be exercised and verified via mocks without needing a real rate-limited API.
behaviors.test_description: Mock the external API to raise a rate-limit exception (e.g., HTTP 429) on the first call, then return a valid result on the second. Mock time.sleep to avoid delays in tests. Call classify(document). Assert that time.sleep was called and that the final result is returned (not an error).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: All paths (rate-limit detection, wait duration, retry, success recovery) can be mocked. An Integration test with a real rate-limited API could be valuable for timing validation, but the core logic is in the module and fully testable at Unit level.
